sentences.text: Actually, nothing made it necessary, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, we had communication from somewhere-- and I don't recall how the news got to us anymore-- that my mother, who was a fairly heavy smoker, and in the bank vaults wasn't allowed to smoke, used the anonymity of the night to come down and to have a cigarette.
The rumor had it that there was no-- no catching of Jews to happen that night, that it was a peaceful night.
Somehow, you heard when danger was supposed to be.
There were always people who had inside information.
My mother allegedly went out to have a cigarette, and walked up and down the building.
I don't know how and who saw her, but this was indeed the giveaway, and the vaults were raided.

We were told when-- my sister and I were told when we were first going to Gustav's home-- and his wife was Helen, and she deserves to be mentioned too-- that in case anything went wrong, my mother wanted to make sure that there is another auxiliary possibility for us to hide.

She had a friend from her school days-- from her secondary school days, or maybe university days, I'm not sure-- who was a doctor, and supposedly a good friend of my mother's and father's, a married man with children himself.
He was the only outsider who knew, other than the person who hid us, that we actually were still there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were getting frantic with the situation of lying still and not being able to do anything.
At that point, I think I have to digress that I think I became unaware, really, what was real and what was imagined anymore.
I had created a dream world for myself, and I realize on looking back that my sort of ability to cope, physically, was because I lived in another world, as did probably my sister.
Our worlds were different, but we created that-- that sanctuary for ourselves.
But there was already encroachment from one world into the other.
We could not sustain ourselves much with what was our safety valve.
And we were terrified, and we sensed that our host and his wife became very nervous.
Once they knew that they were responsible, there was a definite feeling that we could sense of their nervousness, and we decided to call the second string.
We called this doctor.
Actually, Gustav went to tell him that we were-- he admitted that he had been harboring us, and the message was that the choice was ours to make.
There is a possibility for us to hide.
The man was a member of a tennis club, which no longer was in use because the weather was getting cold.
And there was a pavilion there, and the pavilion was fully equipped for guests.
He would make sure that we would-- would have food for a week, that we had plenty of toy material and books, and we were getting very, very carried away about all the carrots.
And that he would find a way of seeing us once a week.
We would be certainly left to our own devices, but we would have much more freedom within that confined situation.
I think it was my decision to make the move.
I don't remember what my sister felt about it, but I just couldn't bear it any longer.
I was shattered that my mother was taken away, and I made the decision.
My sister wasn't against it, but I think it was definitely me taking charge.
In her last letter to me, my mother puts me in charge.
I have that letter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I explained to Gustav why I wanted to go.
I don't think he minded.
I think he was hugely relieved, and I'm not blaming him for it.
And we arranged an evening when this man-- the second one-- would pick us up.
Under cover of darkness, we went just as we were.
We had nothing, really.
We had no more clothes left.
It was very cold.
I-- I remember we wore coats already.
And it was a long walk, and what I do clearly remember was that the streets were extremely busy with uniforms.
It just seemed very obvious that it wasn't an ordinary night.
There were far more arm-banded Slavic Hlinka guards, which was the people that we feared so much, in the streets.
A lot of German soldiers in the streets.
And the man just urged us to walk as fast as we could.
Naturally, we wore no stars, you can imagine.
And we walked for at least 3/4 of an hour 'til we reached this club, which was in a-- AN outer part of Bratislava.
Pretty, and we were absolutely enchanted when we reached the club itself, which was an isolated situation at one corner of a large block of land.
And inside, really, it was paradise.
I mean, we couldn't turn the light on.
We had to be very careful not to flush toilets during the day.
We couldn't run water during the day, but it had, really, a very lush-- compared to where we had been-- luxurious feel to it.
A proper bed for each of us.
And we were absolutely delighted.
There was no fear.
I don't think we were old enough to realize the fact that, when he left us there, we were totally on our own.
It never entered our heads.
This was '45.
I was just starting 14.
No, I wasn't 14 yet.
My birthday's in September.
My sister was 18 months younger.
We made the best we could of our first meal.
We probably ate two days' food in one.
We could still see enough to read, and we fell asleep.
I don't know how much time elapsed before we heard a terrific din outside, and there were a battery of soldiers.
They were Germans.
They were not the Hlinka Guard.
And they were proudly bashing the windows, having sighted us, and torch light, and asking us to open up.
And they said, 'You see?
I told you they would be there.' I recall that comment vividly.
Now, it tormented me for years to come.
Was it a denunciation?
Why did the man bother to take us there if he wanted us to be found?
Was it a way of clearing his conscience that he did his job, but he didn't clear his tracks, and he sort of was seen?
I never found out.
After the war, I saw him once, and I caught him dead, and he disappeared.
I don't know.
He must've perhaps felt too uncomfortable.
He we-- went totally out of sight.
When they came in, they were not rough with us, I must say.
They were hugely disappointed that there were not more of us.
And under cover of night, they were rushing us to the [INAUDIBLE] place where Jews were concentrated and-- and marshaled to, which was the Jewish agency.
I can recall that my sister fell and twisted her ankle, and had great difficulty walking.
And I was terrified that she wouldn't make it.
I couldn't have carried her.
And she was wanting to stop many times, and I was so afraid she would get beaten by them.
And I became quite rough with her to make her go.
We arrived at that agency in the middle of the night.
I think it must've been about 2:00 in the morning or something like that.
And there was a kind of relief.
I really felt whatever.
We are now going to be going somewhere where Mother is too.
I had no idea, none whatsoever-- I don't think any of us did-- what was ahead.
And we were in an enormous room, which was probably like a living room of-- of a large family home.
Full of people.
Packed, absolutely packed.
And we just waited what was going to happen next.
One fellow stood guard outside.
We-- we were allowed to go to the toilet at designated times, and we were in that building for about two or three days until we were taken to the first camp within Slovakia, which was Sered.
There we--
We were together.
And at this point, I must digress.
Everybody had luggage, for better or worse.
At that time, we felt it was terrible that we had nothing.
And, I don't know.
I had these occurrences twice.
This was the first one, in that particular holding camp, that I approached a German soldier and I told him that we have no luggage.

We were told to stick to our luggage because we will need it, and make sure you've got your warm things ready, and all sorts of rather comforting, reassuring talk, all designed to keep us less nervous, I suppose.

But at one point, the very German soldier that I asked to please help me, that I had no luggage at all, actually offered to take me home to the ghetto, where we had not been for a long, long time, so that we could gather some clothing.
He was not much older than I was.
I think he was about 17, in uniform.
And I remember him asking me, 'You're not Jewish, are you?' And I thought, oh, here is an out, the first time it happened to me.
And of course I realized once I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pointed out my sister, there was no possibility of carrying that through.
So I did tell him I was Jewish.
There was no mistake about that.
And he said, 'Never mind.
I'll take you home.' And Vera had to stay back.
She couldn't walk.
Her ankle was very swollen.
And he took me back to the ghetto, where I hadn't been for quite a long time.
And I was terrified of walking in there.
The evidence of my mother's departure was clear.
The flat hadn't been ransacked.
The beds were like beds that people sort of in a hurry left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an extremely, extremely difficult moment.
And I was trying-- he told me that he had very little time.
I was trying to gather a suitcase full of stuff.
And suddenly, I found I was alone in the room.
I didn't know where he had disappeared.
He was standing over me all the time.
And I found him in the second part of the flat, which was another room, bitterly crying.
And that was a very significant moment to me.
He was really, truly horrified what I had to do.
And it was I who had to reassure him that I was all right.
He carried my suitcase, but only to the bottom of the stairs.
He wasn't allowed to, and that upset him very much too.
So we came back, and I feel extremely lucky.
We suddenly had a change of underwear and all the rest of it, and we felt equal to the others and started the journey.
And when we came to Sered which, ironically, was my father's birth place, I really hoped we would meet people and see people, and-- nobody.
They were all strange places.
My fervent hope that I would see Mother there was obviously not realized.
She had been taken away already.
Still, we had no idea what was ahead of us because this was no longer peopled, or guarded, by Germans.
We were fully overseen by the Slovak part of the collaborators.
And they were far worse.
And they kept feeding us the idea that we were going to work.
I guess I was perhaps smarter than-- than I knew, because I thought, what sort of work can we do, my sister and I?
And I asked.
I was smart ass, I suppose.
And he responded and said, you look strong enough, and your sister will go to school.
And I bought it.
How long we were in Sered, I don't remember, but I do know that we were fed.
We had some kind of accommodation, and-- and found a number of acquaintances, and that made it a bit more bearable.
And I have a vivid recollection that probably has nothing to do with anything, but it's part of my bizarre memories.
When one part of the people were being taken to the trains, a friend of my grandmother's-- an old lady-- saw me and called out to me.
She was the wife of the Rosh Hakol of the orthodoxy.
Her fa-- Her husband was no longer alive.
She was there on her own.
And she wore a Tyrolean hat with a long feather-- a quail feather.
She wore hiking boots like you used to wear, thick socks, a leather skirt, and she had a cane with the metal symbols of achieved walks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I can see this woman entering the train, tally hoing to me and saying, we'll meet over there, and sort of trying to reassure herself by reassuring us.
And I thought of all bizarre sights, that probably carried the cake.
Yes.
That's probably what it was because I remember I was very conscious of having missed out on birthday and Rosh Hashanah and all the rest of it.
By-- I was really itchy to get a-- away from there, because I realized my mother was here, but she isn't anymore.
I now want to go where she probably is waiting.

There was a-- a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] roll call one day, and a very senior dignitary came, once again, to check who was there and who was going and who possibly could be bribed by-- or, who could bribe them to stay back.
And he walked through the rows of people and-- and we had to stand very straight.
And when he came to me, he looked me straight in the eyes and said, 'You are not Jewish.
What are you doing here?
Out.' I couldn't understand this, and I was hugely tempted, I admit it.
And I said, 'I have a sister.
Can she come too?' And he said, 'Oh, no, she can't.
That's not your sister.
She is Jewish.
You are not.' I obviously stayed back.
I don't know what his motive was.
I don't know whether charity-- did he want to use me for some better not to be spelled out purposes.
I don't know.
But that was my second strange sort of offer in that regard.
And then, finally, the day came when we were wagoned, and you've heard that story probably many times.
But my story was that we were made to wait on the railway sidings for ages.
It was chaos, absolute chaos.
People screaming and relatives wanting to stay together and not being able to stay together.
And I heard someone calling my name, and saw a very close friend of my parents.
And I thought that was a miracle.
It was a man who was on his own.
His wife was not there.
She had gone with the previous lot.
And he was really delighted to see us.
And he said, 'Stay with me.
I'll look after you.' And my sister was overjoyed.
She was very fond of him.
And we were squeezed in together into the wagon and sat down, and he said, 'Lean on me.' We sa-- we had little space, and he kept saying, lean on me, lean on me.
And as we took off and we realized that we had a bucket that we all had to use and privacy was something we didn't any longer have the luxury of, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the journey began.
There-- there was terrific lot of screaming, and quarreling, and bickering, and nastiness out of pure, sheer despair.
This man was wonderful.
He kept telling us to lean on him and to think that we'd-- with our parents.
He abused the privilege with me, and I was grown up overnight.
He became very tactile during the night, and tried very hard to do something to me that I didn't understand.
And I got very frightened.
My sister was asleep, and I broke free.
There was nowhere to really go, but I wound my way through the wagon to the chagrin and anger and-- and fury of those that were there, and sat in a corner, separately.
I'm telling you this because it's very important to what happened later.
When daybreak came, my sister realized I wasn't there.
And she called me back and I wouldn't come.
And she got very, very angry with me, and she said, 'You always had to be different.
You always were trying to be different from me.' And so it went, like sisters do.
And I just couldn't go back.
I had an ally in the perpetrator because he said to my sister, 'Oh, let her be.
She-- she just wants to be away from us for a while.' And so we actually arrived in what turned out to be Auschwitz at separate ends of the wagon.
Took forever 'til the doors were opened.
By then, the stench and the dreadful characteristics of tested human beings were at the fore, and we were really aware of the fact that we were quite ready to claw at each other.
When the doors were broken open, the first call was 'men out,' so the man was gone.
And I made my way back to my sister, who was not speaking to me.
I couldn't explain.
She was so much of a baby.
I couldn't explain anything to her.
And we left the wagon very, very angry with each other, with the words of my mother ringing in my ears that I had to be the big one and look after her, whatever happened.
And I literally held her hand in a vice.
She was forever trying to break free from me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stood in line-- it was dark-- for a long, long time, and there were very strong overhead lights.
I don't recall-- yes, I do recall the gates of Auschwitz because there was music playing, and I saw the band playing, and I couldn't quite work it out.
They all looked like stone statues, but they were actually making music.
I see the sign, 'work is to free you.' And I was reassured, I suppose.
And I went in with my sister, and held and clutched, and she was practically biting to get free from me.
And then, before we could realize what was happening, we were standing right in front of a most stunning-looking man.
I was aware by then of males.
I've never seen a man as beautiful-- I don't remember-- with several huge Alsatians around him.
And the by-now popular vision of him with the pointer that he used.
And he was pointing in my direction and telling me to go right, and pointing to my sister to go left.
And I just stood.
And he again did that with a very gentle look on his face.
I didn't move without my sister, and she was really wrenching, breaking.
I could-- can't tell you what physical strength was required.
I asked him, 'Can I stay with my sister?' And he said, 'Oh, no, she's got to go to school.
You're going to go to work.' And she turned 'round and said, 'See?' And in a moment, she was free of me, and ran.
And she ran away happily.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]
I guess what happened next helped.
It was so intensive and so incredibly alien [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it kept me too busy to really try and find out what had happened.
And I recall being stripped of that pitiful suitcase.
On my mind, there was only one thing, that I had her things.
How would she get her things?
Well, that didn't take long to be clarified.
And then came the ritual of having head and body hair taken off, and standing in the freezing cold for ages 'til we were marshaled into showers.
I didn't realize why the grownups, so-called, were fighting tooth and nail to walk in there.
I didn't know.
I walked in willingly.
It could've been the gas.
I didn't know anything about it anyway.
But it wasn't.
It was water.
And I recall practically laughing, and thinking of all the bizarre, naked bodies around me, all female.
Other than my mother, probably, I never saw a grown woman naked.
And I was very interested.
I was a very curious child.
I couldn't help comparing body sizes and shapes.
And it was all so bizarrely preoccupying my all the time that I-- the reality didn't hit.
When we were out of there and-- and given gear, which was best to be forgotten, but it looked reasonably clean.
It was very difficult to put on because there were no buttons.
There were no fasteners anywhere that I got.
I didn't understand what was front or back.
Bizarre underwear, bizarre outerwear, and these damned clogs that became the bane of my existence.
And finally, I think what looked like incredibly long spans of time, I stood in front of somebody who was tattooing my arm, and I became, then, a number, a cipher, which is still there.
I was never tempted to remove it because I think it served its purpose in my life.
It reminded me when I thought I was badly done by.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very tall for my age.
I was powerfully built.
And indeed, miraculously, I was taken to a barrack, which apparently belonged to the kitchen staff.
I didn't know.
By that time, I was suddenly terribly, terribly alone and frightened.
I didn't know a soul around me.
And I know that my second shock came when I recognized the face as being the daughter of my grandmother's cook.
Why did I recognize her?
She had hair.
She was one of the unfortunate girls who went there in '42, had lived through the awful apprenticeship and battled through, and was, in fact, in charge.
And she was a capo, which was an office bearer.
I was so happy to see that face.
I thought, oh, well, now everything will change.
A, she will tell me, what is this?
Where am I?
B, she might tell me something about my mother.
I sent word to her through someone that I was in hole in the wall, second on the left, which was the hole in the wall that became mine, shared with four others.

And I think I had a very, very rude awakening when she, a day later, found her way to me only to tell me, 'Who are you?' And when I identified myself and told her of the link, [INAUDIBLE] said 'So bloody what?' and walked away.
And that was my growing-up period.
I worked in the kitchen in Auschwitz, which was hard work, but I was always interested in-- in cooking, and I knew how to handle preparation, and I could peel vegetables.

And I didn't have to go through the beating that some did who couldn't peel fast enough, who didn't know how to cut vegetables, and assured the Germans that they had maids to do it, only to be beaten black and blue on admission of that fact.
I don't know the time, but I do know that it was already very bitterly cold.
Because as part of our kitchen duty, I had to have frequent washings, German style.
I presume the food was-- preparation was for them, because otherwise, why would they have bothered?
And I remember the terror of that Friday-- because it was always on Friday.
And I did mention to you before that I had a skin condition at that stage.
We were being checked every Friday by Dr.
Mengele for our health, which meant having the shower, the dreaded shower, then parading in front of him.
And he had eyes that saw every little blemish.
I befriended someone who was very dear to me, who confided that she was two months pregnant.
Nothing could be seen, but he did see it, and she was taken from me.
That was the last person, more or less, that I had left, who belonged to my sanity.
And I do recall that, amazingly, either he chose to overlook my skin condition-- I don't know.
I have no explanation.
But I passed that dreadful muster week after week.
And we, after the muster, were thrown into the snow for health reasons.
And I remember this terror of being thrown into the snow and then coming in.
So I presume winter was well and truly on the way.
So I was in Auschwitz probably [INAUDIBLE] somewhere-- January or something like that.
And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rumor had it that the front was getting close, and they were gradually taking people away, and I was one of the many that were taken away.
Well, a-- after a long, criss-cross train trip through a fair few bombings, we landed in Ravensbruck, which was to be, indeed, a labor camp.
And there, we went through the same sort of ordeals of registering and endless, endless standing in one spot and nothing happening.
And if somebody collapsed, there was trouble.
And then we were being taken to work in a quarry in Ravensbruck.
This was daily, every day, in the snow, in the quarry work, which was, I think, the hardest time of all.
There was no danger to being gassed there.
They didn't have crematoria.
But the physical work was almost beyond endurance.
Yes.
Yes.
Every day.
And we-- I remember my job was to lift rocks, drag them to a precipice, and roll them down.
And I can assure you, there was nothing at the bottom of that precipice.
It was really Sisyphus type of torture.
And extremely, extremely damaging to our health.
And then, we got back every night to the barracks, had some hot food.
But, incredibly as it may sound, I have more horrific recollection of that period than I had because in its insanity, there was more order.
While I was in Auschwitz, there was more terror.
But there was more order, and the work itself was dextrously-- sorry, was taxing for my dexterity.
But it wasn't arduous like this was, to the point where I felt I can't anymore.
And I think I became pretty, pretty frightened that I wouldn't be able to carry on.
And carry on I did want to.
I don't know why, but I did, because I was still believing that all this is just-- it's not real.
It's just some kind of temporary nightmare.
I think it must've been for about a month.
At the end of that month, the-- the more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fit-looking-- it sounds such a ridiculous statement, but that's exactly what they told us-- were to go elsewhere for work.
And I really thought we were going to be put to death, because I didn't think I looked fit anymore.
But strangely enough, when you've been well built and tall and fit, I guess it took somewhat longer for me to really get debilitated, and I still was considered reasonably fit to go to work.
So the third place was, again, a very small number this time.
And I don't know why I was selected-- I have no idea-- to go to an actual part of German industry.
